title: Dynamical scaling near the pseudogap quantum critical point of the two-dimensional Hubbard model
authors: Mathias Pelz, Gabriel Kotliar, Jan von Delft, Andreas Gleis
abstract: We study dynamical scaling in the quantum-critical fan of the pseudogap-metal to Fermi-liquid transition of the two-dimensional Hubbard model. Using a four-patch dynamical cluster approximation with the numerical renormalization group as a cluster impurity solver, we access real-frequency dynamics over several decades at arbitrary temperatures. Close to the critical doping, the local spin and cluster-current susceptibility spectra exhibit $x=ω/T$ scaling of the form $χ''(ω,T)\sim \tanh(x/2)$, and the cluster contribution to the optical conductivity obeys $Tσ'_{\mathrm{cl}}(ω,T) \sim \tanh(x/2)/x$, implying a $1/T$ cluster dc conductivity. In the scaling regime, the vertex contribution to the cluster optical response is much larger than the bubble contribution. We further find evidence for a marginal-Fermi-liquid nodal self-energy. This, together with the $1/T$ vertex contribution to the conductivity, implies strange-metal optical transport in the quantum critical region. Our results describe several qualitative aspects of several experimental observations.
summary: This paper uses DCA and NRG to show that the 2D Hubbard model exhibits $\omega/T$ scaling and strange-metal transport near a quantum critical point. It demonstrates that the optical response is dominated by vertex contributions and identifies Marginal Fermi Liquid behavior in the nodal regions, providing a theoretical basis for observations in cuprate superconductors.
main_problem: Investigating the dynamical scaling and the microscopic origin of the pseudogap-to-Fermi-liquid transition and the associated strange-metal behavior in the 2D Hubbard model.
main_result: The study finds $\omega/T$ scaling in spin and cluster-current susceptibilities, a dominant vertex contribution to optical conductivity leading to $1/T$ conductivity, and evidence for Marginal Fermi Liquid behavior in the nodal self-energy.
method: Four-patch Dynamical Cluster Approximation (DCA) combined with the Numerical Renormalability Group (NRG) as a cluster impurity solver.
model_or_system: A two-dimensional square-lattice $t$-$t'$-$U$ Hubbard model representing hole-doped cuprates, specifically focusing on the regime near critical doping $p^* \simeq 0.14$.
key_observables: Local spin susceptibility, cluster-current susceptibility, optical conductivity (including bubble and vertex contributions), and nodal/antinodal electronic spectral functions and self-energy.
important_parameters: Critical doping $p^* \simeq 0.14$, temperature scales $T_{FL}$ and $T_{NFL}$, and the scaling variable $x = \omega/T$.
main_assumptions: The self-energy is approximated as momentum-independent within four patches; the results may require more accurate computations to solidify the MFL claim.
figures_summary: Phase diagrams showing $T=0$ transitions; scaling collapses of spin and current correlators; $\omega/T$ scaling of susceptibilities and optical conductivity; and decomposition of current-current correlations by type.
paper_structure: The paper establishes a phase diagram, identifies the non-Fermi-liquid regime, demonstrates dynamical scaling in spin and current responses, analyzes the vertex contribution to transport, and examines nodal/antinodal spectral dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Real-frequency scaling of the local spin susceptibil- ity χ[Sz r ](ω, T) at µ = 1.24. (a) Spectral part χ′′ and (c) real part −χ′, plotted vs. ω for 12 temperatures, indicated by ticks on the color bar. Upon lowering the temperature through the NFL window, χ′′ develops an extended plateau for T ≲|ω| ≲TNFL, while the static response, χ′(0, T), grows approximately logarithmically (inset). (b) The spectral part, plotted as χ′′(ω, T) vs. x = ω/T, shows a scaling collapse onto X ′′(x) = X ′′ 0 tanh(x/2). (d) After subtracting the static part, χ′ shows a scaling collapse, too. Black dash-dotted curves de- pict the scaling functions; dashed lines are guides-to-the-eye.

FIG. 1. (a) Zero-temperature phase diagram extracted from the power-law decay of the imaginary-time local spin corre- lator, ⟨Sz r (τ)Sz r ⟩T =0 ∼τ −γ. The color scale shows the ex- ponent γ versus chemical potential µ and inverse imaginary time τ −1. (Top abscissa: the corresponding hole doping p at T = 0.) Blue regions with γ = 2 indicate Fermi-liquid be- havior at long times. The yellow region with γ ≃1 identifies the intermediate non-Fermi-liquid region emerging near the critical doping p∗≃0.14. Black and white circles denote the crossover energies TNFL and TFL, respectively [106]. The ver- tical dashed line marks µ = 1.24, where we study scaling. (b) Local spin and cluster-current...

FIG. 3. Cluster contribution to the real part of the opti- cal conductivity, σ′ cl(ω, T) = χ′′[jx cl](ω, T)/ω, at µ = 1.24. (a) σ′ cl(ω, T) vs. ω for 12 temperatures, indicated by ticks on the color bar; in the NFL regime, σ′ cl scales as ω−1 (dashed line). Grey curves show the cluster bubble contribution σ′ cl,B, computed from dressed propagators without vertex contribu- tions. (b) Tσ′ cl(ω, T) vs. x = ω/T, showing a scaling collapse onto tanh(x/2)/x (dash-dotted curve). (c) Vertex contribu- tion σ′ cl,V = σ′ cl −σ′ cl,B, plotted vs. ω. In the NFL regime it is orders of magnitude larger than σ′ cl,B, and (d) obeys the same ω/T scaling as the full cluster response σ′ cl.

FIG. 4. The spectral function Ak(ω = 0), computed at µ = 1.24 for (a) the PG phase at T = 10−7t, (b) the NFL at T = 10−3t and (c) the NFL at T = 10−1t. The black dotted line indicates the antiferromagnetic zone boundary. (d) The spectral part of the self-energy at the nodal patch K = (0, 0), Σ′′ (0,0)(ω, T) vs. ω, and (e) Σ′′ (0,0)(ω, T)/T vs. x = ω/T.

FIG. 5. Spectral part of the cluster optical conductivity, σ′′ cl(ω, T), at µ = 1.24. (a) σ′′ cl vs. ω for 12 different temper- atures, and (b) Tσ′′ cl vs. x = ω/T, showing a scaling collapse onto X ′′(x)/x (black dash-dotted curve). Black dashed lines indicate power law behavior.
